Maksimchuk’s multiculturalism in Kursk  26 2.3  A program of social and psychologica l support for the intercultural  adaptation of foreign students  27 2.4  Correspondence between satisfaction and acculturation 28 2.5  Development of the Acculturation Scale to Russia (ASR) 30 2.6  Psychological adaptation in five concepts 30 2.6.1  Stereotypes  30 2.6.2  Social dynamics 30 2.6.3  Dynamics of adaptation 34 2.6.4  Gender  37 2.6.5  Core values 39 3.   ACCULTURATİON AS VALUES WİTHİN ONE WORLDVİEW 41 Conclusion 44 Bibliography 46 INTRODUCTION According to the Ministry of Education of the Russian Federation, there are more than 250 thousand foreign students enrolled in Russian universities . As for the Latin American population: access to university education, a cultural experience overseas, and positive recommendations from fellow countrymen have been key aspects at the time of giving preference to the Russian Federation among many other potential countries.  Therefore, it is significant to understand the phenomenon of the Latin Americans studying in Russian universities and how the image formation of the host country changes along the process of acculturation. Teachers, psychologists and international relations specialists in Russian universities have published numerous works on different topics related with the lifestyle of Latin Americans in Russia. These studies are central to the body of this research, they were compiled and analyzed in order to examine in detail the acculturation process of Latin American students, and the formation of the image of Russia throughout. Thus, we define secondary research as the method used for involving existing data sourced from different channels such as journals, conference proceedings, and webpages of  government institutions such as studyinrussia.ru. Considering the Memorandum of Acculturation stated by Redfield, Linton and Herskovits, the term acculturation refers to the cultural changes originated in the authentic experiences when different cultures get in contact. In a similar way, межкультурная адаптация [mezhkul'turnaya adaptatsiya] in Stefanenko in Ardila et al. , is described as the process of getting into a new culture by gradually adopting its values, norms and customs. Provided that both terms are often used interchangeably, the first chapter aims to describe in detail the theoretical background of the process of acculturation. Otherwise, hereinafter it will be separately recalled as "intercultural adaptation" to denote its specific traits, and concrete contributions made by the authors. Together with it,  we will discuss the peculiarity of student migration to whom access to the trend of internationalization of higher education is in great demand and nevertheless, brings its own particular challenges.  In the second chapter, we will identify the main aspects that influence Latin American students to get interested, immerse, and show (full or partial) acceptance of Russian culture. Among the titles that include the body of literature for this section, it is of prominence the long journey, multiple results and applications in the field taken by Olga V. Maslova since 2005 in everything that has to do with the acculturation process of foreign students regarding aspects such as gender, social  dynamics, stereotypes, personality, among others. In addition, the valuable experiences of students and teachers registered as entries in the university web pages sample testimonies of  first hand key to understand how the process of acculturation is being carried out. Thus, the  current research pretends to shed light on the process of acculturation of Latin American students as a whole process that is inextricably intertwined with the image of Russia.  It is believed that as long as the process of acculturation goes smoother, the image of Russia created by the students is more positive as well, and vice versa.  Main objective: To describe the acculturation process of foreign students in Russia Specific objectives: To explore the similarities and differences in contrast perceived by the Latin American students in Russia. To uncover the peculiarities of the acculturation process of Latinoamerican students and how these shape the image of Russia along. To examine the process of image formation and its potential as a tool for understanding the acculturation process of foreign students. Image is understood as a construct of the worldview which is in permanent change and comes as a result of the interrelation of the factors involved when acculturating .   T he current work is written in 51  pages, a total of  30  references are cited throughout the research paper. Key words: Acculturation, Intercultural adaptation, Latin American students. Social dynamics, Russian culture, Ethnic identity, Cultural distance, Core values and beliefs,  Intercultural Competence, Межкультурная Адаптация, Этнокультурная компетентность,  Коммуникативный этностиль, Базовые убеждения личности, Культурный шок, Этническая идентичность. Анотация Преподаватели, психологи и специалисты по международным отношениям российских университетов опубликовали множество работ на различные темы, связанные с образом жизни иностранных студентовв России. Эти исследования занимают центральное место в рамках данного исследования, они были собраны и проанализированы с целью детального изучения процесса аккультурации латиноамериканских студентов и формирования образа России во всем мире. Таким образом, мы определяем вторичное исследование как метод, используемый для привлечения существующих данных, полученных из различных каналов, таких как журналы, материалы конференций и веб-страницы государственных учреждений, таких как studyinrussia.ru . Принимая во внимание Меморандум об аккультурации, изложенный Редфилдом, Линтоном и Херсковицем, термин "аккультурация" относится к культурным изменениям, возникающим в результате аутентичного опыта при соприкосновении различных культур. Аналогичным об разом, межкультурная адаптация  у  Стефаненко, Ардилы и др. описывается как процесс вхождения в новую культуру путем постепенного принятия ее ценностей, норм и обычаев.  Во второй главе мы определим основные аспекты, которые влияют на то, чтобы латиноамериканские студенты заинтересовались русской культурой, погрузились в нее и продемонстрировали (полное или частичное) принятие. Среди названий, которые включают в себя основную литературу для этого  раздела,  выделяется    длинный путь , многочисленные результаты и приложения в этой области", проделанный Ольгой В. Масловой с 2005 года во всем, что связано с процессом аккультурации иностранных студентов в отношении таких аспектов, как гендер, социальная динамика, стереотипы, личность., среди прочих.  Таким образом, текущее исследование претендует на то, чтобы пролить свет на процесс аккультурации латиноамериканских студентов как на целостный процесс, который неразрывно связан с образом России.  Считается, что до тех пор, пока процесс аккультурации проходит более гладко, образ России, создаваемый студентами, также является более позитивным, и наоборот.  Основная цель: Описать процесс аккультурации иностранных студентов в России . Конкретные цели: Изучить особенности восприятия латиноамериканскими студентами культурного контраста между родной страной и Россией . Раскрыть особенности процесса аккультурации латиноамериканских студентов и то, как у них формируется образ России. Изучить процесс формирования образа России и его потенциал как инструмента для понимания процесса аккультурации иностранных студентов. Образ понимается как элемент мировоззрения, которая находится в постоянном изменении и возникает в результате взаимосвязи факторов, задействованных при аккультурации . Текущая работа написана на  51  страницах, в общей сложности в исследовательской работе приводится  30  ссылок.  Ключевые слова: Acculturation, Intercultural adaptation, Latin American students. Social dynamics, Russian culture, Ethnic identity, Cultural distance, Core values and beliefs,  Intercultural Competence, Межкультурная Адаптация, Этнокультурная компетентность,  Коммуникативный этностиль, Базовые убеждения личности, Культурный шок, Этническая идентичность. Chapter one: On Acculturation and Intercultural adaptation The Memorandum of Acculturation stated by Redfield, Linton and Herskovits  in 1936 explains the term of acculturation referring to the cultural changes originated in the authentic experiences when different cultures get in contact. On the other hand, Stefanenko cited in Ardila et al. in 2019  explained межкультурная адаптация [mezhkul'turnaya adaptatsiya] as the process of getting into a new culture by gradually adopting its values, norms and customs. In the light of the above, we are to describe in detail the theoretical background of the different approaches analyzing the local view on the intercultural adaptation of foreign students in Russian universities, and the global issues of acculturation. . 1.1 Maslova’s overview: from ethnic identity to core values in acculturation  Intercultural adaptation of Latin American students has been profoundly researched by Maslova, O. V.  dating back her first studies to 2005 .  The author highlights the differences of ethnicities among the  students as a starting point for the students themselves to raise awareness about the peculiarities of their culture and in turn, to see the traits of their host country: Russia.  In the light of the above, a process of intercultural adaptation will be provided with better scenarios for a real apprehension by the foreigners of the local culture. In the first place, it is of importance the relationships that foreign students can establish in the Russian society.  In order to start interacting socially, stereotypes come first as they bring some ideas about how the other probably is. Maslova points out that Latin Americans are characterized by locals for being enthusiastic, communicative and optimistic, so these traits are pretty much idealized, playing in favor of the process of foreigners getting acquainted with Russians. Also, social relationships ranging the scope of friends, sentimental partners, and colleagues play a significant role in acculturation. According to the results, foreign students complained about the ill will and the lack of sympathy perceived from Russians towards them. Language and also, indifference and in some cases, even the feeling of disrespect towards foreigners are common issues in the first months. Anyhow, Maslova had a weighty argument in this regard, emphasizing on the ways of communicating in both cultures. Thus, aspects such as being openly expressive, smiling, observing patterns of hierarchy if any, are judged  according to the own cultural background, and it is expected that foreign students take into account at the same time, the cultural background of the other.  Ramelli M, Florack A. et al in 2012  elaborated on the formation of acculturation orientations. Acculturation was thought to be long-term  affected by perceptions of communication efficacy upon arrival and early friendships with locals during the first few months after moving to a new country. In addition to reporting back on the successful communication upon arrival and early friendships, the researchers asked participants to identify their present attitude toward contact with the receiving society and cultural maintenance. As expected, there was a significant correlation between the current attitude toward engagement with the receiving society and views of good communication efficacy upon arrival and friendships with members of the receiving society during the early phase in the new culture. Additionally, current cultural maintenance was negatively correlated with early friendships with members of the receiving society. Regarding the dynamics of adaptation studied by Maslova  in relation to the variables of personal satisfaction, interaction, conformism, depression, nostalgia and  the level of cultural estrangement, we should keep in mind that the main goal of the students is to obtain a degree, that is why partial adaptation of certain manners and behaviors entail a certain level of conformism enough for coping with the academic duties in the frame of the adaptation process. All this comes in contrast to a real process of acculturation in which the student apprehends the new milieu of the new culture. As for conformism being a strategy to get immersed into the new culture, it contrasts with rejection followed by the feeling of nostalgia. This is a gap between the values of the former and the new culture in which the student takes distance and  decides not to adopt the local culture. From this point of view, there is adaptation of certain manners tending to conform, or to hold onto one's own culture giving room to the feelings of strangeness and depression due to the new socio-cultural conditions. Also, the subject of gender plays a significant role due to the differences in preference and the cycle of adaptation. For the first period, conformism is the most common attitude while the student is adopting norms, rules, and “doing what pleases others”. For the time later,  as it is shown in the studies of Maslova O. that not all Latin American women keep on conforming. A second stage in acculturation represents the adoption of values, which is not preferred and it is better to keep the own values. What is surprising is that foreign students claim to get better acquainted with the own culture by contrasting it with the new culture. Through the time, nostalgia for the values of the former culture increased the level of estrangement, making Latin American women become conservators and even promoters of their culture in Russia. On the other hand, Latin American men who are fewer in number, experience more critical moments at the beginning, but through the time they try hard to adopt manners, behaviors and all what it takes to immerse in the culture. Their level of conformism  keeps stable, and their self satisfaction is related with the closer acquaintance of the mindset  of the new culture helping at the same time to decrease their level of estrangement. Thus, in order to overcome the different difficult moments,  women rely on their spiritual beliefs, family, and goals set at the beginning of the journey to Russia. As for men, they rely on their friends and their fellow countrymen living at the moment in Russia. Maslova’s hypothesis is based on the core beliefs  of the students as a way to analyze their level of conformism and the chances for them to adapt to the new culture. In other words, if the world seems supportive, the students  rely more on their capabilities, thus they will handle better with adaptation and, further with their acculturation. If students rely on the kindness of the people they are surrounded by and their level of self esteem is high, the student will feel less estranged from the new culture, looking to adopt the norms and the values of the new community. On the other hand, if the world does not seem fair, the self esteem of the student is less favorable, and depression takes place. On balance, active correspondence with the foreign students in order to get feedback about their experience in general in Russia, and knowing the peculiarities of their cultural background makes part of a favorable process of acculturation. As well as, the awareness of the students themselves and the aspects that it entails to acculturate. In other words, to move from the stage of conformism to adoption of some values, which is recalled by Maslova as consciousness about the core beliefs. Coupled to this framework, it is possible to think about the idea of different roles such as the one of temporarily abandoning one's own culture in order to get a new one, overimposing the first culture by  leaving aside the new culture, or becoming an intermediary who is moving between both cultures. In any of the cases, Maslova highlights the key contribution of the university and the country, to be informed and support the student in her/his process of acculturation.  1.2 The socio – psychological view on Intercultural adaptation The hypothesis of Maksimchuk E. D. in 2018  explained in her doctoral dissertation  relies on  the idea of  training the universities’ personnel to effectively help foreign students according to the particularities of their cultural background and better cope with the social-psychological challenge they face. The framework of Maksimchuk’s multidisciplinary approach to the topic of intercultural adaptation of foreign students is understood as a complex process including aspects such as cultural shock, identity crisis, health, self realization, etc.  For the author, working with foreign students entails an individual approach in which their personality entails the social and psychological components of their lives at the university and externally. Universities should guarantee the students’ growth, self realization, self organization and, it should be an individualized work. This will help the program participants to realize the correction of their subjective ideas, giving them the opportunity to change the direction of their behavior, and also form  an adequate level of ethnic identity and better control the situational anxiety. In a similar way, Maslova and her hypothesis on the core beliefs explained above highlights the ethnic approach for acculturation. Actually, the approach of Maksimchuk expands and addresses more specific psychological traits that in her consideration seem to directly act positively upon the intercultural adaptation of foreign students. Further explanation of the ideas exposed by these authors will be sampled in chapter two. 1.3 Intercultural competence grounded in philology and pedagogy Grebennikova I. A.  asserts that intercultural competence is basic for  the adaptation of the foreign student conditioned by matching his/her character with the new culture as a system of values, taking into account its cultural peculiarities in the learning process.  It is precisely Maksimchuk  who models a socio-psychological program of accompaniment founded on the conceptual positions of Grebennikova: the principles of humanism, socialization, intercultural  competence, and communicability. The result of her work provides a scientific view on cultural shock, ethnic identity, ethnic tolerance, the scope of cultural distance, degree of motivation to study, intercultural competence and self realization. It is of importance the emotional state of the students in order to address each of these variables.  In everyday life, its progress on the social and everyday life skills, work on the socio psychological particularities of the students overcoming negative emotions, and development of intercultural competence what helps to carry on better with their adaptation.  According to the author, the development of Intercultural competences is based on the acquaintance and analysis of the mentalities of the new socio-cultural milieu, on the basis of its background information. This assertion matches the view that Maslova has, since this acquaintance is taken from the own cultural background and mentality brings conflicts between the known and the unknown, the own and the foreign, the will to understand and the prejudices . In the words of Grebennikova, adaptation of the foreign students to the new culture comes as a result of the understanding of the mentality of other cultures, where one can see the acquaintance with the background information of that culture, the master of the language, behave him/herself according to the communicative situation and interact with the representatives of those cultures.  The concept of background information for the most part refers to such things as the social and racial origins, the financial status, or the type of work experience that a person has. It also entails the history of a country, governmental organization, subjects of material culture, folklore which characterize  the ethnicity of a community, a culture owned by each representative of the culture, and reflected in the language spoken by the community. Grebennikova’s work  as cited in Maksimchuk  is related in specific to the program of pedagogical assistance as the basis for accompanying the adaptation of foreign students. This area of work ensures that foreign students are aware of the socio-cultural characteristics and values of the culture of the host country. As criteria for the adaptation of foreign students, the researcher highlights: their acceptance of the values of the new community, their integration into the social and cultural life of the host country, adaptability, and positive interpersonal relationships with the classmates. One way to see how adapted a person is is the acquaintance with the cultural peculiarities of the new society, the master of the language, and the skill to decode  the obtained information. It is precisely communication that is the main way for a teacher and  student to interact, know the culture of each other, and many cultures since in a foreign group of students there are usually a variety of cultural backgrounds, which may also challenge the perception of the speakers.  Once again, it is in the classrooms indoors and outdoors, that the acquaintance of the culture of the language takes place, and it is the educational process by means of communication, where the student can grasp the main principles and parameters of the communication in a multicultural milieu, where it is language and all what it consists of, which helps the student to interact in the new socio-cultural milieu. A clear sample from the perspective of the universities is that, there are special faculties in charge of the training programs for foreign students. As for the South Ural State University , one can see an accurate planning and design of the program of language learning. It combines different learning activities in formal and informal contexts. Concerning The Linguistics Institute and the International Communication Department, they both agree on the importance of combining an academic life which in broad terms stand for attending the preparatory course for two semesters, together with competitions related with Russian language and culture. E.g. The celebration of The Day of Slavic Written Language, rethinking a pedagogical approach based on the idea of discovering a new world focusing not on the difficulties but rather, the interest of getting acquainted with cultural aspects inner to the study of a language. All the aspects considered both in the fields of linguistics and psychology lead to the idea of promoting situations in which there is  the practice of a real language bearing a communicative character, and a cultural value within the schemes of the possible relationships of the immediate community where the foreign student belongs to.  1.4  Acculturation Vs. Intercultural adaptation At the first stage, the concepts of "intercultural adaptation" and "acculturation" were used by researchers as synonyms which eventually led to a certain confusion of concepts. In the words of Maksimchuk E.  acculturation was considered as a one-way road, passing through which all immigrants came to the same result. Today, most modern researchers believe that the process of intercultural adaptation, first of all, involves adaptation to new socio-cultural conditions in which the temporary migrant or visitor finds how his/her personality is in transition as well. At the same time, it is absolutely not a prerequisite to deny your native culture in favor of a new one. Redfield   R. ,  Linton   R. and  Herskovits   M.H.   first proposed the concept of acculturation in 1936. They argued that the process of acculturation begins after a group enters into a sufficiently prolonged direct interaction with one or more groups from other cultures. The result  of such interaction required changes in the behavioral patterns of all participants. Within their research, the following are analyzed:   Types of contacts:  1. Where the contacts are between whole groups or they are between an entire population and groups selected from another population. 2. Where the contacts are friendly or hostile. 3. Where the contacts are between groups of approximately the same size, or between groups of markedly different size. 4. Where the contacts are between groups marked by unequal degrees of complexity in material or non-material aspects of culture. 5. Where the contacts result from the arrival of the culture carriers to the habitat of the receiving group; Situations in which acculturation can occur: 1. Where elements of culture are forced upon a people, or are received voluntarily by them. 2. Where there is no social or political inequality between groups. 3. Where inequality exists between groups, in which case any of the following may result: political dominance by one group, without recognition of its social dominance, political and social dominance by one group and recognition of social superiority of one group by the other without the exercise by the subject group of political dominance by the former; And the process of acculturation: 1. Selection of traits under acculturation; 2. Determination of traits presented and selected in acculturation situations; 3. Integration of traits into the patterns of the accepting culture. As a result of their research, the authors found the following: Acceptance  is the assimilation of a significant part of another culture and the adoption not only of certain behavior patterns but to the inner values of the culture with which they have come into contact.  Adaptation  is the combination of original and borrowed elements into a functional harmonious whole preserving contradictory attitudes that interact in everyday behavior according to certain circumstances.  Reaction  is the emergence of many different counter–acculturation movements in which the leading role is played by psychological factors. These latter work either as compensations for an imposed or assumed inferiority, or through the prestige which a return to older pre-acculturative conditions that may bring to those participating in such a movement. As Smolina T. L.  in Maksimchuk documented the fact that such a definition differs from a simple change in culture, which is rather an aspect of acculturation and assimilation. Through the time, acculturation  began to be interpreted as the changes in culture that arise as a result of the interaction of two or more cultural systems. Currently, the term of acculturation is used less often by researchers to denote adaptation processes. However, since it essentially reflects any entry of an individual into a new socio-cultural environment, it is common to find authors that use it as a synonym for socialization in relation to culture. In the contemporary world, researchers try to distinguish between individual and group acculturation. To denote individual acculturation, the concept of psychological acculturation is introduced. Psychological acculturation is understood as those changes that occur as a result of an individual's interaction with other cultures and as a result of an individual's participation in the acculturation process through which her/his ethnic group experiences. It is noticeable that Berry J. proposed a model of acculturation, and Maksimchuk   explained that his theory is of great value while it connects acculturation with stress models. This allows the author to affirm that, as a result of a successful course of the acculturation process, a person receives a positive experience, that is, to get the necessary skills and abilities for effective stress management.  Actually, It is throughout the very process of an individual's intercultural interaction  with a new cultural environment that the sources of acculturation problems are found, and not in a specific culture. On the other hand, Berry J.  identifies two parameters that determine the details of the process of entering a new culture. These parameters assume polar responses (positive or negative). An exhaustive examination of the responses by two parameters determines the strategy of acculturation of the migrant's personality. It is necessary to emphasize that the choice of a strategy for entering a new culture depends not only on the peculiarities of multicultural interaction and the cultural characteristics of the new host country, but also on the objectives of the stay of visitors (migrants) in the new socio-cultural conditions. For example, people arriving at a permanent place of residence often use an assimilation strategy. In addition, the assimilation process does not result in excessive difficulty for those who receive vocational education in another country. However, internally displaced persons experience psychological difficulties associated with the breakdown of ties with their homeland, which slows down the assimilation process. In one of his researches Belaya E. N.  points out that it is recent the idea of assimilation as the most acceptable acculturation strategy. However, today the main goal of acculturation is the integration of interacting cultures, which will contribute to the formation of a multicultural personality. Taking Intercultural adaptation and Acculturation in parallel, it can be said that mainly these two terms have been describing the changes in culture that arise as a result of the interaction of two or more cultural systems. As for the description of individuals, Serkova V. A.  succeeds to synthesize it as follows: Acculturation itself is the process of regulating the flows of the contents of culture from the center "the self". It is "oneself" who accumulates certain cultural codes of management, hierarchizes cultural processes and the production of meanings. Then, Acculturation is primarily a form of self-knowledge, self-understanding, self-identification and self-presentation. Chapter two: The experience of Latin Americans in Russia The increasing level of competitiveness in the global market for the provision of captivating programs, and the peculiarity of student migration to whom access to the trend of internationalization of higher education is in great demand brings its own particular challenges. For example, the issues of psychological adaptation explained in the previous chapter proved to be of relevance in the process of acculturation, and necessarily request an individualized approach for different groups of foreign students. In order to discuss those challenges that foreign students encounter, and how teachers, administratives and students themselves perceive this situation, and provide their own insights; this chapter is an analysis of  sources such as: scientific articles, articles and entries of the university web pages and magazines, related specifically to Latin American students enrolled in Russian universities. 2.1 I. A. Grebennikova’s idea of communication and its application in Siberia Krasnoyarsk manages online and offline courses of Russian language through various educational approaches. They have a compendium of different materials, and multiple formats to study Russian having their own textbooks, an imaginary radio station ”Kanikuli FM”, and etc. One of the traits that make the Federal Siberian University  (FSU) stand out it is its approach focused on regional  studies of Siberia in Russia, the study of linguistics and everyday language. Its materials in the form of radio broadcasts and standardized language testing on Russian as a foreign language were allocated a subsidy from the Ministry of Education recognizing the development of updated programs and attractive materials for foreign students of Russian Language at FSU. The efforts made by the FSU resonate with the hypothesis of Grebennikova  on integration as the most successful strategy of acculturation. Owing to the characteristic of updating the materials, creating new channels of communication, and setting up situations of interaction closer to the everyday Russian language needed for the everyday life of the foreign students, one can see that intercultural communication is the path for locals and the foreign students to foster cultural integration being active members of the interchange of the own and the other’s values.  2.2 E. D. Maksimchuk’s multiculturalism in Kursk  The development of Intercultural competences is based on the acquaintance and analysis of the mentalities of the new socio-cultural milieu. And this is precisely the diversity that the author describes for a  regular group of foreign students enrolled in the preparatory courses of Russian universities across the country. Also, the diversity that in the case of Maksimchuk is hold by the federal  subjects of the Russian Federation. The ethnic composition of these regions is transforming every year due to increased migration flows (economic and social migration). It is pointed out by the author that the Kursk region is one of the multicultural regions of the Russian Federation and, according to statistical data, an increase in the level of the multicultural composition of the population of the Kursk region is observed every year.  Having said this, it is relevant to raise awareness about the diversity of different levels as a chance to develop intercultural competence. 2.3 A program of social and psychological support for the intercultural adaptation of foreign students  The attempt of Maksimchuk  in creating a complex approach which could work in a ethnic mixed group of 15 to 20 students takes into consideration most of the meaningful factors and particularities of the process of intercultural adaptation. The author considered the  relevance and  developed a program of social and psychological support in intercultural adaptation of foreign students, described its goals and objectives, created a logical structure, a conceptual foundation, and the  methodological principles, the conditions of implementation, and the methods and forms of work.  Her study on the foreign students coming to Russia gave as a result two  portraits: a social one and a psychological one, both presented as structural components. Three main directions of psychological support for international students are chosen and described: the development of social and household skills of foreign students, the development of social and psychological characteristics of foreign students and overcoming negative phenomena and, the formation and development of intercultural competence of foreign students. Her study gave as a result the detailed description of two profiles: social and psychological. The social profile entails the unpreparedness for the experience of being alone in new socio-cultural circumstances, underdeveloped skills to organize one's activities, cultural distance with partners with whom interact -local and other foreign students, insufficient level of development of intercultural competence, limited contacts within the social setting. The psychological profile of the foreign student who has just arrived to Russia is the following: a formed and stable attitude towards the future profession, a steady interest in the subject area and the desire to fully master the language of the specialty, an increased level of anxiety as well as fear of the new conditions in the environment that are perceived as threatening, a state of psychological tension of uncertainty and frustration, relationship between ethnic tolerance and ethnic identity, the need for communication through which self-knowledge of new socio-cultural reality is carried out. 2.4 Correspondence between satisfaction and acculturation The study of Aguilar  involved 145 undergraduate and graduate students, who came to study in Russia from 11 Latin American countries (from Ecuador 86 respondents, from Colombia - 21, from Bolivia - 11, from Peru - 10, from Dominican Republic - 4, from Mexico and Venezuela - 3 each, from Guatemala, Nicaragua and Chile - 2 each, from Costa Rica - 1 person). Women made up 55% of the sample. The average age of study participants was 25 years. The youngest respondent was 19 years old, the oldest was 40. The average time spent in Russia was 3.7 years.  All of them were students and postgraduates of various Russian universities, and lived in different Russian cities (Moscow, St. Petersburg, Rostov-on-Don, Belgorod, Kazan, Ufa).  Significant indicators of the levels of positive adaptation in Russia were: «Мне нравится путешествовать по разным местам России», «Я люблю русскую природу» и «Я веду себя в соответствии с нормами и правилами России». In contrast with the low indicators «Мне нравится русская еда», «Я знаю много русских песен» и «Я хотел бы жить в России много лет».  It is concluded that the level of satisfaction of Latin American students studying in Russia is directly related to the level of their  acculturation and intercultural adaptation. Therefore, even for those students who came to Russia solely for education, an important role plays their development of intercultural competence. 2.5 Development of the Acculturation Scale to Russia (ASR) The study covered 445 international students (42% female) from 71 countries. For psychometric analysis checking the external validity, it was concreted the method of expert evaluations (16 experts are teachers of the Russian language, basic disciplines, tutors on educational work) and correlation analyses were used. As a result, the proposed Acculturation Scale to Russia (ASR) has a high consistency based on a three-factor structure of cognition, behaviors, and emotions. This scale was created to assess the level of acculturation of international students in Russia.  2.6 Psychological adaptation in five concepts Maslova undertook the following variables: stereotypes, social dynamics, dynamics of adaptation, gender and core values for describing the psychological adaptation.  2.6.1 Stereotypes  The results of the first variable -stereotypes show that the image is more idealized towards foreign students than their own fellow countrymen in the classroom, they turned out to have a positive image of a Latin American student, especially if it is taken into account how highly the joy of life, sociability and optimism are valued in Russian culture. 2.6.2 Social dynamics The highest degree of acceptance among foreign students was found in relation to representatives of Latin America. The social distance of Russian students to Latin Americans is significantly shorter in comparison with foreigners of other nationalities (in particular, from Africa and China): the majority (67%) of survey participants do not object to closest forms of interaction - to accept a representative of Latin America as a spouse; 79% of respondents are ready to see a friend in a Latin American.  However, despite such a generally positive attitude towards Latin Americans on the part of citizens of the host Russian society, it was recalled the study of Intercultural adaptation of Latin Americans in Russia, conducted by Kuyala Makera G. E. under the guidance of Maslova, and it was revealed that this group of foreign students, among the main difficulties of their adaptation, notes the unkindness and the cold attitude towards them from Russians. This study involved 80 Latin American students who enrolled at RUDN   from different countries of Latin America (Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Bolivia, México, Panamá, Costa Rica, Venezuela, Guatemala, Nicaragua). Half of the respondents (40 people) were students of the preparatory faculty, the average period of their stay in Russia was 7.5 months. The other 40 people were senior students, whose average stay in Russia was 3.5 years. It was designed the questionnaire: "Adaptation of a personality to a new socio-cultural environment" and it was used the methodology of unfinished sentences. According to the results of this study, the greatest difficulty in the early stages of adaptation for young people from Latin America is the ignorance of the language (practically none of the students speaks Russian at the time of arrival in Russia). In second place among all the difficulties, Latin American students are dissatisfied with the attitude of Russians towards them. Russians are perceived by them as unkind, rude, aggressive and hostile towards each other and especially towards foreigners. They appear to them as cold, insensitive, indifferent, with no respect for foreigners. In the opinion of Maslova, one of the reasons for this perception of Russians and the attitude towards them, is that there are differences between the Russian and Latin American communication styles. Russian communication style is more direct (in terms of expressing communicative intentions), categorical (when expressing opinion, advice, refusal), focused on the middle and low levels politeness (in symmetrical and top-down relationships) and at the same time focused on a high level of politeness in relations “from the bottom up”, status-oriented (asymmetrical roles, inequality of communicants). Due to the manner of Russian people to speak loudly and categorically, often without respect for politeness (for example, interruption is allowed) Latin Americans, at first poorly understanding or not understanding Russian at all, think that everyone around them is shouting and swearing (among themselves and on them), which often even scares them. In addition, the Latin American communicative style is over expressive and verbose, while Russian is expressively moderate and laconic, which also gives  Latin Americans grounds to assume that Russians are cold, insensitive and without different. Features of non-verbal communication of Russians reinforce the impression about their coldness and unkindness. Both foreigners and researchers note that Russian people smile much less often than people from other cultures. The faces of Russians in the crowd are concentrated, often gloomy, closed. The “everyday non-smiling” of Russians is usually explained mainly by the fact that Russians smile in those situations where they really experience positive emotions. A smile serves to convey emotional information. Smiling in Russia must be meaningful and have emotional reasons, it must express feelings. In Russia, a smile is an expression of a natural, sincere disposition and sympathy for “one’s owns” (friends, acquaintances, but not the whole world!), joy about a particular event. The Russian smile has nothing to do with etiquette. A smile is not a necessary element of communication, like in many countries. In Russia, people don't just smile a little, here they just do not. It is not customary to smile at strangers on the street. A smile can be taken as a sneer or as a sign that there is some kind of absurdity, for example, in clothing, or even as an appeal for sexual content. Our compatriots (especially young people) after traveling around the world and acquaintances with other cultures notice this feature of theirs - unsmiling, tightness and try to be more smiling, but this is not always easy for them to succeed at. Even a polite smile is not a habit for Russians, even for those who works in the service industry. Therefore, Latin Americans are shocked and disturbed when, in their words, “salesmen in the shops - as if stony and deaf, heard - did not hear,  understood - did not understand. The absence of any signs from the side service sector workers, especially in the first months of the stay of Latin Americans in our country, is a big obstacle for them to adapt, although they usually get used to it later on. Not understanding the situation, they interpret it as "indifference" to them by those around them. Most often, such a situation develops on the street, when “everyone runs past” and Latin Americans it seems that "no one wants to answer" and "no one wants to help". Here are some examples of specific statements: “Cold people and cold weather”, “Everything is dark and matte in this city, it lacks the sun", "Cold and little friendly  people, sad and cloudy atmosphere”, “Russians were so cold and it was, it's so hard to find Russian friends", "People are very rude and indifferent". «Холодные люди и холодная погода», «Все темное  и матовое в этом городе, ему не хватает солнца», «Холодные и мало любезные люди, грустная и облачная атмосфера», «Русские были такими холодными, и было так трудно находить русских друзей», «Люди очень грубые и безразличные». Latin Americans perceive the communicative style of Russians taking as a starting point their worldview, the etiquette of their country, and therefore the seriousness and unsmilingness of Russians shocks them, they see behind it not only unkindness, but also a hostile and cold attitude towards oneself. In other words, behind the facade of Russian seriousness and unsmiling, Latin Americans do not notice a positive attitude towards themselves and interpret the seriousness, unsmiling Russians as indifference and even hostility. In turn, Russians perceive communicative style of Latin Americans from the standpoint of their culture, and it seems to them that cheerful, life luster, smiling Latin Americans “never do not lose heart”, “always have fun” and “enjoy life”.  Behind the smile they see a constantly cheerful joyful state and do not see the emotional difficulties experienced by the Latin American students in their process of adaptation. 2.6.3 Dynamics of adaptation The following indicators were compared: adaptation - personal satisfaction, interactivity, conformity, depression, nostalgia and alienation in students of the preparatory faculty and senior students obtained with the help of a questionnaire. The results of these indicators are shared in the form of Graphic 1 here below. The data obtained indicate that female and male Latin Americans do not have a sense of belonging to a new society and involvement in it, personal satisfaction with relationships with those around them is quite low, there are no significant differences in adaptation between groups of students of the preparatory faculty and senior courses. Statistically significant differences were noted only on a scale of alienation. Gender differences were revealed in the dynamics of this indicator: the estrangement of female Latin Americans grows over the years, and the estrangement of male Latin Americans, on the contrary, decreases. The level achieved by the end of the first year of study adaptation is practically preserved in subsequent years. At one of the levels, which is far from optimal (because satisfaction is low) - there is a stagnation of indicators, which can be explained by the fact that acquaintance with the norms and rules of behavior of a new society and their development is faster than acquaintance and acceptance of values. The behavioral level “do it like everyone else”, being more external, is mastered faster (already at the preparatory faculty), and the level of values, affecting  more deep personal structures, begins to be mastered much later, and this happens with great difficulty. In addition, the main goal of foreign students is to get an education, so the motivation for immersion and acceptance of a new culture is lacking, which means that in this case it is more legitimate to speak of an external adaptation, carried out mainly by conformity, and not about genuine adaptation, in which compliance with the new cultural environment is achieved.   We called these identified combinations of adaptation parameters "conformist" and "nostalgic" according to the configuration (combination) of the above parameters adaptations (adaptation - personal satisfaction, interactivity, conformity, depression, nostalgia and alienation). Significant differences related to three indicators of adaptation: conformity, nostalgia and aloofness. Graphic 2 joined hereby below represents the results of both styles (conformist and nostalgic. Students with a higher conformity, score indicators of nostalgia and alienation were significantly lower than in the second group. Their index of depression was also lower, but at the trend level. Those having the conformist style are more oriented towards new norms and rules, more actively strive to comply with them and apply them in practical life than supporters of the nostalgic style. Not surprisingly, they turn out to be less alienated from the new environment and less hard going through a break with the usual values. Proponents of the nostalgic style are more likely to experience disunity with their traditional values, are more adherents of their own culture, less willing to conform to the new culture and more distant from it. In general, it can be said that conformists are more prosperous in terms of adaptation than adherents of the usual culture: they are lacking adaptation - alienation and depression. Graphic 1. Dynamics indicators of Intercultural Adaptation of Latin American students Graphic 2. Adaptation styles of Latin American students 2.6.4 Gender  Analysis of the data obtained showed that at the first stages of adaptation, the vast majority of Latin American students (95% of girls and 75% of boys) adheres to a conformist style, i.e. actively learn the rules and regulations of the new culture, applying the common principle of intercultural interaction - "do as others do, do as they like." However in senior years, only half of the girls adhere to the conformist style, while most boys still prefer it. In other words, in the early stages of adaptation, when there is an acquaintance with the external aspects of culture, female Latin Americans are more conformist, oriented towards the assimilation and acceptance of these norms. However, later on, when meeting with the deeper components of culture, primarily with values, Latin American women have the feeling of rejection and desire to preserve their former, native, familiar value system. When meeting another culture, a better understanding of one's own occurs. Girls often note that “here I have become more value my country and my people”, “now I love my country, I used not to care". Over the years of their stay in Russia, they have an increasing (at the level of a trend) feeling of nostalgia for their usual values, alienation increases from the new society. Trying to keep their old world, the girls become defenders of the old traditional values and "conservatives" of their former experiences, thereby trying to create in Russia its own “little” Latin America. The adaptation strategy of male Latin Americans differs in many respects from the strategy of their fellow countrywomen. For them, the initial period of adaptation proceeds more rapidly, more critically, but since they are fewer than girls, they are fixed on the usual rules and interact more actively with the surrounding society, "dissolving" in a new environment, it is legitimate to talk about their more complete penetration into a new culture (many have Russian girlfriends). They are more active, more bold continue to master this culture, and their desire to follow the rule "do it as others do, do as they like” is not reduced. Their personal satisfaction depends on the degree of assimilation of the new society and is associated with overcoming alienation from the new culture. Gender differences also manifested themselves when answering the question about the sources of support in the difficulties of adaptation: female Latin American more often than boys note that they were helped to cope with difficulties by their faith in God, their family, the presence of a goal and faith in oneself. Friends and compatriots often act as a source of support for male Latin Americans. Thus, the adaptation to the new socio-cultural environment of male and female Latin American who come from traditional cultures is different. Gender acts as one of the adaptation factors. The culturally prescribed social role influences the degree and modes of social activity in the new culture: the frequency of contact, the manifestation of initiative, the style of behavior in conflict, etc. 2.6.5 Core values As for Latin American students in Russia, their psychological adaptation  is characterized by their conformity (shown statistically at a significant level in the Graphic 3 hereby below) connected with their basic beliefs: the more favorable the world seems and the more faith in themselves and their abilities, the stronger Latin American students want to conform to the new scenario and play by its rules. The more the students believe in the kindness of the people surrounding them, the more they are pleased with themselves, the less alienated they are from  the new scenario, and the more the students strive to master the norms and values of the new society. The less fair the world seems and the lower the students appreciate themselves, the more depressed they are.        Indicators of the Scale of Core Beliefs from students of five regions To sum up, diversity becomes a key aspect for locals and foreigners so it is actually a trait present in everyday life which also undertakes traits within individuals. Giving consideration to diversity as the possibility to enrich the worldview of the self and the other, it is key to bear in mind the cultural integration as the  scenario in which communication and intercultural competence are the tools for creating points of contact between cultures. Similarly, language and communication were the two main drawbacks of the processes of acculturation reported in this chapter.   Chapter three: Acculturation as values within one worldview The attempt to analyze and offer alternative solutions to cope with the intercultural adaptation of foreign students in Russia was reported in detail within the two previous chapters. Consequently, it can be said that it is mainly awareness about the methodological aspects in the areas of pedagogy, linguistics, sociology, and psychology which have researched and provided the needed scenarios for the acculturation of the students to take place in the best possible conditions. As a result, based on the whole experiences informed by the students, and the corresponding work made by each specialist, it represents a specific approach for understanding how the student can better face the inconveniences of her/his adaptation. It could be said that there are different levels of adaptation including the adoption of certain manners and behaviors, and  also the need of acquiring new values to see the world.  Anyhow, everything that reaches the scope of the topic of values from both sides: Russia as the host country and, the student as the guest, and the approach of each reflecting one worldview, serving as a starting point to see oneself and others results threatening in different ways at different points in time of the acculturation. This is precisely what it will be discussed in this chapter. The process of acculturation that requests foreign students to undertake certain manners and behaviors looking forward to adopting specific traits of lifestyle e.g. greeting adults and children in different ways, increases the level of acquaintance of the local culture, making the knowledge of Russia and its language a value. Thus, through the  time it will be visible that adoption sets the path for adaptation as it is the case of the well-known saying ´do in Rome as the Romans do´. In this respect, the foreign student may not face a stressful moment as much as the new manner or behavior do not overlap with the manners or behaviors of her/his cultural background.  However, the effort for becoming skillful enough with the mastery of Russian language and the understanding of the situations in which the foreigner may address one way or the other may not stop there. On a different level, the foreign student should go further and deeper in the acquaintance of the local culture bringing the topic of values and beliefs. It is perhaps the most crucial element of culture since discerning what is good in one's own culture might turn out to have a different hint in the new context. In the same line, it can be realized that certain values embed specific beliefs. Usually individuals in a society have particular beliefs, but they also share collective values. As it is well-known, The International Women’s day and the belief of flowers as the regular gift. In spite of the knowledge on the celebration, and the statements of congratulations, it should also be observed among many aspects, the value of social distance in the local culture and the belief that making a present may happen only between men and women to show an interest in a potential romantic relationship. Secondly, flowers play an important role in Russia as for growing the concept of a local Flower Culture taking into account a broad range of meanings about flowers and their amount, type, color, packing, etc. Thus, one can say that those objects rich in meanings as flowers are, they are also subjects of study to learn some of the values and beliefs of the local culture.  In the same way, one should elaborate around the concept of cultural diversity. As opposed to monoculture, or a homogenization of cultures, it is the variety, one of the aspects that makes a community rich. Indeed, all what it has a social, ethnic, religious background in interaction with other cultures will bring forward many discussions desirable for the deeper understanding of the self and the other, and the agreements to make in the framework of tolerance and appreciation of the difference. So cultural diversity can be taken as a common value that must be observed and given a personalized approach for giving chances of getting to know each other, while preserving the individualities. Seeing that bringing cultures together is a complex reality, although one of the most important aspects to keep in mind in the process of acculturation, the lens through which it can be more clear the whole universe of my own culture, it is the universe of another culture. So as much as it is guaranteed a dialogue of cultures through the prism of acculturation, there will be more opportunities to follow the natural  changes of the own and the other’s worldviews within the sets of values and beliefs.  Conclusion Taking the ideas developed throughout the current research, it can be concluded that the changes in culture that arise as a result of the interaction of two or more cultural systems are the starting point of a process of acculturation for both sides. Thus, it is of utter importance to think about the roles played by each member of the community hosting a foreign student for whom communication and culture become the aspects to identify her/himself, and the new culture.  Along the process of acculturation, awareness of the similarities and differences are the first step for students to ponder over their identity as members of a foreign culture in Russia. While it seems like common sense and an obvious idea, those changes informed, supported and guided by professionals as the teachers and psychologists of the universities, will enhance the beginning of a positive cultural experience while living in Russia.  As for the peculiarities of the acculturation process of Latin American students and how these shape the image of Russia along, it is clear that stereotypes of locals towards this ethnicity in specific may help the students to find more situations of interaction, and in this way more experiences to get in contact with the local culture. Thus, their image of Russia will be nourished according to their everyday life and how they cope with it. Namely, the topic of gender regarding female Latin American students who seem to adopt manners and behaviors of locals faster in contrast to male students whose intercultural adaptation starts in general with the satisfaction of achieving goals after mastering at  some level the Russian language, and being able to interact at some point. On balance, the analysis of the acculturation depending on the gender of the Latin American students brings differences towards the appreciation of Russian culture. Needles to say, the differences in the image built along the process of acculturation  The integration of cultures entails the interaction of multicultural personalities of groups and individuals. All this acknowledges the diversity and the need for having a personalized approach to different ethnicities anchored in a deep acquaintance of the self and the others. Partly, we can rely on the sum of aspects presented in the studies as it is clear the scientific rigor analyzing and designing an extended, grounded and varied methodology for students, teachers, and all the community carrying processes of acculturation. It is clue to develop a cultural competence on the basis of the scope of the identities of the ethnicities, and the corresponding core values. Probably with this in mind, cultural integration is to happen in the frame of tolerance and true value of diversity. In regards with the process of image formation, it can be said that it is nourished by the development of his cultural competence even in the very first moment when it comes the idea of studying in Russia. At that point, all those issues relating the journey to start question the student about her/his will to undertake such an entrepreneurship,  and all what it is known or ignored about Russia. From that very moment, one can see how the image of one country changes, and the beginning of the former state of the process of acculturation. Bibliography Ardila, A. Цели:
1. Исследование особенностей межкультурной адаптации иностранных студентов.
2. Определение факторов, влияющих на процесс адаптации.

Задачи:
1. Анализ литературы по теме межкультурной адаптации иностранных студентов.
2. Исследование особенностей адаптации студентов разных национальностей.
3. Определение роли языка в процессе межкультурной адаптации.
4. Анализ возможностей изучения русского языка для иностранных студентов.
5. Исследование проблем, возникающих при адаптации иностранных студентов в России.
6. Определение мер поддержки иностранных студентов в процессе межкультурной адаптации.
7. Анализ теорий этапов кросс-культурной адаптации.
8. Исследование вклада профессора Т.Г. Стефаненко в развитие этнопсихологии в РУДН.
9. Определение роли социальной дистанции в межкультурной адаптации.
10. Исследование особенностей межкультурного взаимодействия в образовательном пространстве.
